label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's read the passage again carefully to find out why Babbage failed to continue his research in 1828. The passage mentions that when Babbage returned to London in 1828 after a long holiday abroad due to health issues, he was "at the end of his resources", which means he had almost no money left. It also says many bills remained unpaid, and his assistants and co-workers quarreled with him and left with expensive tools. For one year, no work was done. So, the main reasons cited are his poor health (which led to a break in work), financial problems (he ran out of money), and conflict with co-workers. However, the question asks for the main reason why he failed to continue in 1828. The passage states that when he returned in 1828, he was "at the end of his resources" - this is a strong indication that money was the main issue. The fact that many bills remained unpaid and co-workers left with expensive tools suggests financial issues. While his health did break down earlier (before 1828), the passage implies that by 1828, the main problem was financial. Looking at the options: (A) he was in poor health - This was a problem earlier, but when he returned in 1828, the passage focuses more on financial issues. (B) he almost ran out of money - The passage directly states he was "at the end of his resources" in 1828. (C) his co-workers argued with him - While this happened, it's mentioned as a consequence of his financial problems. (D) he spent all his money on his bills - The passage says bills remained unpaid, not that he spent it all. Based on
